q: What is the difference between a serial and a concurrent dispatch queue in GCD?
a: A serial queue executes one task at a time, in FIFO order, so each task finishes before the next begins. A concurrent queue can run many tasks simultaneously on multiple threads, though they still start in FIFO order. The key insight is that both dequeue work in order, but a concurrent queue doesn't wait for one to finish before starting the next. You reach for serial queues to protect shared state and concurrent queues to maximize throughput on independent work.
d: Both queue types pull work items off the queue in FIFO order, but only serial queues guarantee one-at-a-time execution and thus a happens-before relationship between items. A concurrent queue hands work to the thread pool and lets the system decide how many threads to spin up, bounded by available cores and the pool limit. A common misconception is that a serial queue equals one fixed thread — it does not; GCD may run successive blocks on different threads, it just guarantees they never overlap. The main queue is a special serial queue bound to the main thread. Concurrent execution order of completion is nondeterministic even though start order is FIFO. For mutating shared state, a serial queue gives you mutual exclusion essentially for free, which is why it's the classic lock replacement in pre-actor Swift.
f: Trap: Does a serial queue always run all its work on the same thread? No — GCD only guarantees non-overlapping, ordered execution, not thread affinity. The exception is the main queue, which is pinned to the main thread.

q: Explain the difference between the main queue and the main thread.
a: The main thread is the actual OS thread where UIKit and AppKit must run, and where the app's run loop lives. The main queue is a serial `DispatchQueue` that is guaranteed to schedule all of its work onto that main thread. So they're related but not identical: the queue is a scheduling abstraction, the thread is the execution resource. Code dispatched to the main queue always ends up on the main thread, but you can technically be on the main thread without going through the main queue.
d: `DispatchQueue.main` is a serial queue whose blocks are drained by the main run loop on thread zero. `Thread.isMainThread` checks the actual thread, whereas there's no public API to ask 'am I currently executing a block from the main queue' — and those aren't equivalent questions. For example, code running inside a `CFRunLoop` source or a UIKit callback is on the main thread but wasn't dispatched through the main queue's `async`. `@MainActor` in Swift Concurrency is built on a global executor that maps to the main thread, but it uses an enqueue mechanism that can avoid a redundant hop when already on the main actor. A subtle gotcha is that `DispatchQueue.main.sync` from the main thread deadlocks because the queue is serial and busy. Understanding this split matters when bridging Swift Concurrency and GCD, since `MainActor.assumeIsolated` reasons about the actor, not the raw thread.
f: Trap: If `Thread.isMainThread` is true, is it safe to assume your code was scheduled via the main queue? No — you can be on the main thread from a run-loop source or a synchronous callback without ever touching the main queue.

q: What are QoS classes and how do they relate to priority inversion?
a: Quality of Service classes — like `.userInteractive`, `.userInitiated`, `.utility`, and `.background` — tell the system the intent and urgency of work so it can allocate CPU, scheduling priority, and energy accordingly. Priority inversion happens when a high-priority task is blocked waiting on a resource held by a lower-priority task that the scheduler isn't running. GCD mitigates this with priority donation: when a high-QoS item is queued behind or waits on lower-QoS work, the system temporarily boosts the lower work's priority. So QoS isn't just a hint about speed; it's central to keeping the right work unblocked.
d: QoS maps onto thread scheduling priority and timer coalescing, CPU throughput, and I/O throttling. Priority inversion is dangerous because a `.background` task holding a lock or sitting ahead in a serial queue can stall a `.userInteractive` task, causing visible hangs. GCD resolves this through QoS propagation and override: submitting a higher-QoS block to a serial queue, or calling `sync` from a high-QoS context, raises the effective QoS of the queue's currently running and pending work. Using a `DispatchSemaphore` to wait defeats this because the runtime can't see the dependency, so no donation happens — a classic source of inversion. The fix is to express dependencies in ways the runtime understands (`sync`, target queues, async/await) rather than opaque blocking primitives. Asymmetric QoS across a producer/consumer boundary is a frequent real-world bug.
f: Trap: Does blocking on a `DispatchSemaphore` get priority-donated like `sync` does? No — the runtime can't see the semaphore dependency, so a low-QoS holder won't be boosted, which is exactly how inversions sneak in.

q: How do you implement a reader-writer lock using a dispatch barrier?
a: You create a concurrent queue and perform reads with a normal `async` or `sync`, so multiple readers run in parallel, while writes go through `async(flags: .barrier)`. The barrier flag makes the write wait until all in-flight reads finish, runs the write exclusively with nothing else executing, and only then lets queued reads resume. This gives you concurrent reads but serialized, exclusive writes — the classic reader-writer pattern. It's a lightweight way to protect a shared resource without a full lock.
d: Under the hood, a `.barrier` work item submitted to a concurrent queue acts like a fence: GCD drains everything before it, executes the barrier block alone, then resumes concurrency. Reads should typically use `sync` so the caller gets the value back synchronously, while writes are usually `async(flags: .barrier)` to avoid blocking the caller. A correctness subtlety: the barrier only provides exclusivity on that specific queue, so every access to the protected state must funnel through the same queue, or the guarantee is void. Using a barrier on the global concurrent queues does nothing useful because those are shared system queues — you must own a private concurrent queue. Reads done with `async` rather than `sync` can return stale values relative to a just-issued write if you're not careful about ordering. In modern Swift, an `actor` expresses the same intent more safely, though an actor serializes reads too unless you mark accessors `nonisolated` over immutable data.
f: Trap: Does a `.barrier` flag work on `DispatchQueue.global()`? No — global queues are shared, so barriers are ignored there; you must create your own private concurrent queue for the pattern to hold.

q: What is a DispatchGroup and when would you use it?
a: A `DispatchGroup` lets you track a set of asynchronous tasks and get notified when all of them complete. You call `enter()` before starting each unit of work and `leave()` when it finishes, then either `notify` to run a closure asynchronously on completion or `wait` to block until done. It's the go-to GCD tool for fan-out/fan-in — kicking off several independent network calls and reacting once they've all returned. The crucial discipline is balancing every `enter` with exactly one `leave`.
d: Internally a group is essentially a counter; `enter` increments and `leave` decrements, and the group is 'empty' at zero, which triggers `notify` blocks or unblocks `wait`. When you submit work with `queue.async(group:)`, the enter/leave is managed automatically; the manual `enter`/`leave` pair is for callback-based APIs you don't control. The most common bug is an unbalanced count — an early return or error path that skips `leave` causes `notify` to never fire and `wait` to hang forever, while a double `leave` crashes. `wait(timeout:)` returns a `DispatchTimeoutResult` so you can detect stalls. Groups don't impose ordering or mutual exclusion; they only count completion, so shared state still needs its own protection. In Swift Concurrency, `withTaskGroup` and `async let` supersede this with structured guarantees and automatic cancellation propagation.
f: Trap: If you call `leave()` more times than `enter()`, what happens? It crashes — over-leaving traps because the group's count would go negative, which is treated as a programmer error.

q: How does a DispatchSemaphore work and why is it dangerous with async/await?
a: A `DispatchSemaphore` holds a counter; `wait()` decrements and blocks the calling thread if the count goes below zero, while `signal()` increments and wakes a waiter. People use it to limit concurrency or to turn an async callback into a synchronous wait. The danger with async/await is that semaphores block a real thread, and the Swift cooperative thread pool has a small, fixed number of threads. If you block those threads waiting on a semaphore, you can starve or deadlock the pool because the work that would call `signal()` may itself need a pool thread that's now blocked.
d: Swift's cooperative thread pool is sized to roughly the core count, and its fundamental contract is that tasks suspend rather than block — `await` frees the thread to do other work. A semaphore violates this by parking a thread, removing it from the pool entirely. The classic deadlock: you `wait()` on a pool thread, and the `Task` responsible for `signal()` can't get scheduled because no thread is free. Even when it doesn't deadlock, it causes thread starvation and priority inversion, since the runtime can't reason about the hidden dependency. There's also a correctness trap: never let a semaphore deallocate while its count is below its initial value, or it crashes. The right tools for bounding concurrency in Swift Concurrency are task groups with a sliding window, `AsyncSemaphore`-style actor implementations, or simply structured concurrency limits — anything that suspends instead of blocks.
f: Trap: Is it safe to wrap an `await` call with a semaphore to make it synchronous on the main actor? No — blocking the main thread on a semaphore while awaiting can deadlock and freezes the UI; you must restructure to stay async.

q: What is a DispatchWorkItem and what does it offer over a plain closure?
a: A `DispatchWorkItem` wraps a block of work as a first-class object you can submit to a queue, but it adds capabilities a bare closure lacks. You can cancel it before it starts, attach `notify` blocks to chain follow-up work, and `wait` on its completion. A common use is debouncing — you create a work item, schedule it with `asyncAfter`, and cancel the previous one if a new event arrives before it runs. So it's the GCD primitive for cancelable, observable units of work.
d: Cancellation on a `DispatchWorkItem` is cooperative-ish but coarse: `cancel()` only prevents execution if the item hasn't started; once running, it won't be interrupted, though `isCancelled` lets the block check and bail early. You can also assign QoS and flags at creation. `notify(queue:execute:)` lets you build small dependency chains where a completion block fires on a chosen queue after the item finishes. A subtlety is reuse — a work item can be submitted only once meaningfully; re-submitting an already-executed item is a misuse. For the debounce pattern, you must hold a reference to the pending item so you can cancel it, which means it usually lives as a property. In Swift Concurrency, a `Task` plus `Task.cancel()` covers the same ground with finer cooperative cancellation.
f: Trap: Does calling `cancel()` on a running `DispatchWorkItem` stop it mid-execution? No — cancellation only blocks an item that hasn't started; a running item must check `isCancelled` itself to bail out.

q: Why does calling sync on the current serial queue deadlock?
a: `sync` submits a block and then blocks the calling thread until that block finishes running. On a serial queue, only one block can run at a time, so if you call `sync` from within a block already executing on that queue, the queue is busy with you and can never start the submitted block. The calling thread waits for the block, the block waits for the queue, and the queue waits for the caller to finish — a circular dependency. This is one of the most common GCD deadlocks.
d: The deadlock arises specifically because `sync` couples thread-blocking with queue scheduling on a serial resource. It's most infamous as `DispatchQueue.main.sync { }` called from the main thread, which instantly freezes the app. With concurrent queues, `sync` on the same queue usually doesn't deadlock because another thread can pick up the work, though re-entrant `sync` patterns can still cause subtle issues. GCD does not track queue identity to detect this and bail with an error — it simply hangs — so it's a silent failure. A historical mitigation is `DispatchSpecificKey` to tag a queue and check, before calling `sync`, whether you're already on it. The lesson is that `sync` is a reentrancy hazard, which is part of why actors and async/await, with their suspend-don't-block model, are safer.
f: Trap: Does `DispatchQueue.global().sync` from a global queue deadlock the same way `main.sync` does? Not necessarily — concurrent queues have multiple threads, so the work can run on another thread; the deadlock is characteristic of serial queues.

q: What is thread explosion and why does GCD limit its threads?
a: Thread explosion happens when you submit lots of blocking work to concurrent queues, and GCD keeps spawning new threads to service items because the existing threads are stuck blocked rather than progressing. Each thread costs memory for its stack and adds scheduling and context-switch overhead, so spawning hundreds of them degrades performance and can exhaust resources. GCD's thread pool has a hard cap — historically around 64 per QoS — precisely to bound this. The real fix is to avoid blocking threads at all, which is exactly what async/await's cooperative model enforces.
d: GCD grows the pool when a queue has runnable work but all current threads are blocked, on the theory that a new thread can make progress. The pathology is recursive or fan-out blocking — for instance, dispatching `sync` calls that block while waiting on other dispatched work — which makes GCD overcommit. Beyond the per-QoS soft limit there's a global thread ceiling, and hitting it can deadlock the whole process. Symptoms include ballooning thread counts in Instruments and severe latency under load. Swift's cooperative thread pool was designed specifically to prevent this: it caps threads near the core count and relies on suspension (`await`) rather than blocking, so a waiting task yields its thread instead of holding it. This is why mixing blocking primitives like semaphores into async code reintroduces the exact problem the new model was built to solve.
f: Trap: Does increasing the QoS or using more concurrent queues fix thread explosion? No — more queues share the same pool and blocking still spawns threads; the only real fix is to stop blocking threads.

q: How does async/await suspend a function under the hood?
a: When an async function hits an `await`, it can suspend, meaning it saves its state into a heap-allocated structure and returns its thread back to the cooperative pool instead of blocking it. The work it was waiting on, when ready, schedules a continuation that resumes the function from where it left off — possibly on a different thread. So suspension is not blocking; the thread is freed to run other tasks. This is what lets a tiny pool of threads handle huge numbers of concurrent operations efficiently.
d: The compiler transforms an async function into a state machine; each potential suspension point becomes a resumption entry. Local state that must survive the await is stored in the async frame, an allocation in the task's storage rather than a thread stack, which is why async stacks are 'split'. At an `await`, control returns to the runtime, the thread is recycled, and when the awaited operation completes it enqueues the continuation onto the appropriate executor. Resumption may occur on any thread of the cooperative pool unless isolation (like `@MainActor`) pins it. Crucially, not every `await` actually suspends — if the result is already available, it may continue synchronously — but you must always assume it might. Because the thread can change and other code can run during the suspension, any invariants held across an `await` can be violated, which is the root of actor reentrancy concerns.
f: Trap: Does crossing an `await` always mean the function suspends and switches threads? No — `await` is a potential suspension point; it may complete synchronously on the same thread, but you must never rely on either outcome.

q: What is structured concurrency and how do async let and withTaskGroup express it?
a: Structured concurrency means child tasks have a well-defined scope tied to their parent: they're created within a scope, and the scope can't exit until all children complete or are cancelled. `async let` spawns a child task whose result you await later in the same function, perfect for a fixed number of concurrent operations. `withTaskGroup` is for a dynamic number — you add child tasks in a loop and iterate their results. Both guarantee that cancellation and errors propagate down the tree and that no task outlives its scope, which prevents leaks.
d: The defining property is the task tree: a parent awaits its children, so lifetimes nest. `async let x = f()` immediately starts a child task and binds a placeholder; the actual suspension happens when you `await x`, and if you exit the scope without awaiting, the child is automatically cancelled and awaited implicitly. `withTaskGroup` gives explicit control: you `addTask`, then consume results via the group's `AsyncSequence` conformance, and the closure can't return until all tasks finish. Errors in a throwing group propagate and the group cancels remaining children. Child tasks inherit the parent's priority, task-local values, and actor isolation context, unlike `Task.detached`. This nesting is what makes cancellation reliable and avoids the orphaned-work problem that plagued completion-handler code.
f: Trap: With `async let`, does the work start when you write the binding or when you `await` it? It starts immediately at the binding; the `await` only retrieves the already-running result, so two `async let`s run concurrently before either is awaited.

q: What is the difference between Task and Task.detached?
a: A plain `Task { }` inherits context from where it's created: the current actor isolation, the priority, and task-local values. `Task.detached { }` inherits none of that — it runs with no actor isolation, default priority unless specified, and a fresh task-local scope. So if you create a `Task` inside a `@MainActor` method, it runs on the main actor, whereas a detached task would not. You should default to `Task` and only reach for `Task.detached` when you deliberately need to escape the current context, which is rare.
d: Inheritance matters for correctness and performance. Because `Task` adopts the enclosing actor's isolation, a `Task` started in a `@MainActor` context stays on the main actor without explicit annotation, avoiding hops and keeping you safe to touch isolated state. It also inherits priority, so it won't accidentally run at a lower urgency than its initiator, and it copies task-local values like trace IDs. `Task.detached` severs all of this; it's an unstructured, top-level task useful for genuinely independent background work that shouldn't be bound to the caller. A frequent misuse is reaching for `Task.detached` to 'get off the main actor', when the cleaner approach is to call a `nonisolated` async function or move work to an actor. Neither form is structured with respect to the creating scope — both are unstructured and must be cancelled or awaited manually if you hold the handle.
f: Trap: Does `Task { }` created in a `@MainActor` function run off the main thread to avoid blocking the UI? No — it inherits main-actor isolation, so it runs on the main actor; use a `nonisolated` async API or an actor to actually offload.

q: How does task cancellation work in Swift Concurrency?
a: Cancellation is cooperative — calling `cancel()` on a task doesn't forcibly stop it; it just sets a flag that the task is expected to observe. Your code checks `Task.isCancelled` or calls `try Task.checkCancellation()`, which throws `CancellationError` if cancelled, and bails out gracefully. Many built-in async APIs, like `Task.sleep` and `URLSession`, check cancellation for you and throw automatically. The model trusts code to clean up and stop on its own, so long-running loops should check periodically.
d: Cancellation propagates down the structured task tree: cancelling a parent cancels all children, and a thrown error in a task group cancels siblings. It's purely a signal — there's no preemption — so CPU-bound loops that never check the flag will run to completion regardless. `Task.checkCancellation()` is the throwing convenience; `Task.isCancelled` lets you do custom cleanup like returning a partial result. For resources that need teardown when cancelled, `withTaskCancellationHandler` runs a handler synchronously at cancellation time, which is essential for bridging callback APIs. A subtlety: the cancellation handler can run concurrently with the operation, so it must be thread-safe. Cancellation state persists, so once cancelled, subsequent `Task.sleep` calls throw immediately. Detached tasks are not cancelled by their creator's scope, since they're unstructured.
f: Trap: Does cancelling a `Task` immediately stop a tight `for` loop doing computation? No — cancellation is cooperative; without an explicit `Task.isCancelled` check the loop runs to completion.

q: What is an actor and how does it guarantee data-race safety?
a: An `actor` is a reference type that protects its mutable state by serializing all access through a private executor — only one task can run an actor's code at a time. From outside, every call to an actor's isolated members is asynchronous and must be `await`ed, because the caller may need to wait its turn. This synchronization is enforced by the compiler at compile time, not just convention, so you can't accidentally touch the state concurrently. The result is mutual exclusion without manual locks.
d: Each actor has an executor and a conceptual mailbox; incoming calls are enqueued and processed serially, which is why cross-actor access is async. Internally this is far cheaper than a lock in the contended case because it integrates with the cooperative scheduler — a task waiting for the actor suspends rather than blocking a thread. The compiler enforces isolation: non-`Sendable` state can't escape, and you can't synchronously read an actor's `var` from outside. `nonisolated` members can run without taking the actor's executor, useful for immutable or computed-from-constant data. Actors are reentrant, which is both a feature and a footgun. Compared to a serial `DispatchQueue`, an actor gives compiler-checked isolation and non-blocking waits, but it also allows interleaving at suspension points, which a serial queue running synchronous blocks does not.
f: Trap: Since an actor serializes access, is its state fully protected across an `await` inside an actor method? No — at each `await` the actor can run other queued calls, so invariants held across the suspension can be broken by reentrancy.

q: Explain actor reentrancy and the classic bug it causes.
a: Actors are reentrant, meaning that when an actor method hits an `await` and suspends, the actor is free to start processing other queued calls before the original method resumes. The classic bug is a check-then-act across an await: you check some state, await something, and assume the state is unchanged when you resume — but another call may have mutated it in between. For example, a caching method that checks 'is this already loading?', awaits the network, then stores the result can end up doing duplicate work or overwriting. The fix is to re-validate state after every await or to avoid awaiting while holding an invariant.
d: Reentrancy exists deliberately to prevent deadlocks — if actors were non-reentrant, an actor awaiting its own re-entrant call would hang. The cost is that you cannot treat an actor method as atomic; only the synchronous spans between suspension points are atomic. The duplicate-request bug is canonical: two callers both see 'not cached', both kick off the fetch, and both write. A robust fix stores an in-flight `Task` in a dictionary so the second caller awaits the existing task rather than starting a new one. More generally, re-check preconditions after `await`, and design so that mutations happen in synchronous regions. People often mistakenly believe actors give transactional atomicity across awaits; they emphatically do not. Recognizing suspension points as places where 'anything could have happened' is the core senior-level mental model.
f: Trap: Can you assume a value you read before an `await` in an actor method is still valid after the `await`? No — reentrancy means another call may have mutated it; you must re-read or re-validate after the suspension point.

q: What does @MainActor guarantee and how is its isolation enforced?
a: `@MainActor` is a global actor that guarantees the annotated code runs on the main thread, serialized with all other main-actor work. You can apply it to a type, a method, a property, or a closure, and the compiler ensures that access from outside main-actor context is `await`ed and that you can't touch main-actor state from an arbitrary thread. This is the type-safe replacement for manual `DispatchQueue.main.async` around UI updates. The guarantee is checked at compile time, so violations are errors, not runtime crashes.
d: A global actor is a singleton actor whose isolation can be applied broadly via an attribute; `@MainActor`'s executor schedules onto the main thread. When you call a `@MainActor` function from a non-isolated context you must `await`, which may hop to the main thread; from already-on-main-actor code the call is synchronous. The compiler propagates isolation through inference — a class annotated `@MainActor` makes its members main-isolated by default. `nonisolated` opts specific members out, for example a `Sendable`-safe computed property over constants. A common optimization detail: the runtime can elide the hop when already on the main actor, so `@MainActor` isn't always a thread switch. Note that `@MainActor` isolation is about the actor, not raw threads — `MainActor.assumeIsolated` lets synchronous code assert it's already on the main actor to call isolated APIs without awaiting, trapping if the assertion is false.
f: Trap: Does `@MainActor` always cause a thread hop when you call into it? No — if you're already executing on the main actor the call runs synchronously with no hop; the hop only happens when crossing from another isolation domain.

q: What does nonisolated mean and when do you use it?
a: `nonisolated` opts a member out of its enclosing actor's isolation, so it can be called synchronously from anywhere without `await` and runs without taking the actor's executor. You use it for members that don't touch mutable isolated state — like computed properties over immutable constants, or protocol conformances such as `Hashable` and `CustomStringConvertible` that must be synchronous. It's also how you expose work that genuinely doesn't need serialization. The compiler still forbids a `nonisolated` member from accessing the actor's isolated mutable state.
d: Because actor-isolated members are implicitly async to outside callers, conforming to synchronous protocols requires `nonisolated` — for instance, `var id: UUID { ... }` over a `let` can be `nonisolated` so it satisfies `Identifiable` without async. The restriction is that `nonisolated` code can only read other `nonisolated` or immutable members; reaching for an isolated `var` is a compile error. There's also `nonisolated(unsafe)`, an escape hatch that disables the checking for a specific declaration when you're manually guaranteeing safety — useful but dangerous. On the main actor, `nonisolated` lets you define helpers that run off the main thread. A subtle point with Swift's evolving model is that `nonisolated` async functions historically ran on the caller's executor or a generic one; newer rules (under approachable-concurrency / SE proposals) adjust where nonisolated async functions execute, so the exact thread can depend on language mode.
f: Trap: Can a `nonisolated` method read an actor's isolated `var` if it's just a quick read? No — it cannot access isolated mutable state at all; only immutable or other nonisolated members are reachable without awaiting.

q: What is Sendable and what makes a @Sendable closure special?
a: `Sendable` is a marker protocol that signals a type is safe to pass across concurrency boundaries — between actors, into tasks, across threads — without introducing data races. Value types of `Sendable` members are automatically `Sendable`; classes generally aren't unless they're immutable or internally synchronized and marked `@unchecked Sendable`. A `@Sendable` closure is one the compiler guarantees can be safely run concurrently, so it must only capture `Sendable` values and cannot capture mutable state by reference. The compiler enforces these rules so you catch races at compile time.
d: `Sendable` conformance is checked structurally: a `struct` or `enum` is implicitly `Sendable` if all stored properties are; a `final class` with only immutable `let`s of `Sendable` type can be. `actor` types are always `Sendable` because they synchronize internally. `@Sendable` on a closure tightens capture rules — captured variables must be `Sendable`, and you can't capture a `var` you mutate, which prevents shared mutable state escaping into another thread. APIs like `Task.init` and `withTaskGroup.addTask` require `@Sendable` closures. `@unchecked Sendable` tells the compiler 'trust me', appropriate when you protect state with a lock yourself, but it's an unsafe assertion. Under strict concurrency checking (Swift 6 mode), non-`Sendable` types crossing boundaries become hard errors rather than warnings. The mental model: `Sendable` is about safe transfer, not about whether something is thread-safe to use concurrently in place.
f: Trap: Is a `final class` with `let` properties automatically `Sendable`? Only if every stored property is itself `Sendable` and immutable; one mutable or non-Sendable property breaks it and you'd need synchronization plus `@unchecked`.

q: What is the precise difference between a data race and a race condition?
a: A data race is a specific low-level phenomenon: two or more threads access the same memory location concurrently, at least one of them is a write, and there's no synchronization — this is undefined behavior. A race condition is a broader correctness problem where the result depends on the timing or interleaving of operations, even if every individual access is properly synchronized. So all data races are bugs, but you can have a race condition with zero data races. Swift Concurrency's safety guarantees eliminate data races, but it cannot, by itself, eliminate all race conditions.
d: Data races are about memory safety and are formally undefined behavior in the language model — the compiler and CPU may reorder or tear reads and writes, producing nonsensical values or crashes. Race conditions are about logical ordering: for example, two atomic increments that are individually safe can still produce a wrong total if the read-modify-write isn't itself atomic, or a check-then-act that's stale because state changed between the check and the act. Actor reentrancy bugs are race conditions, not data races — the accesses are serialized and race-free, yet the logic is wrong because of interleaving across awaits. Swift 6's strict checking proves the absence of data races at compile time via `Sendable` and isolation, which is a strong guarantee, but you still have to design logic to avoid timing-dependent incorrectness. Conflating the two leads engineers to assume that 'no data races' means 'thread-safe logic', which is false.
f: Trap: If Swift 6 strict concurrency compiles with no warnings, are you guaranteed correct concurrent behavior? No — it guarantees no data races, but race conditions like check-then-act across awaits remain possible and are your responsibility.

q: How does the compiler enforce Sendable across isolation boundaries?
a: The compiler tracks isolation domains — actors, the main actor, and non-isolated contexts — and whenever a value crosses from one domain to another, it requires that value's type to conform to `Sendable`. Passing arguments into an actor method, returning values out, capturing in a `@Sendable` closure, or sending into a `Task` are all boundary crossings that get checked. If you try to send a non-`Sendable` type, you get a compile error under strict checking or a warning in the transitional mode. This static analysis is what lets Swift claim compile-time data-race safety.
d: Enforcement is a flow-sensitive analysis layered on the type system. Each function and closure has an inferred or declared isolation, and the checker reasons about which domain a value originates in versus where it's used. Sendability is checked at the crossing point, not at declaration, so a non-`Sendable` value used entirely within one actor is fine. Swift refined this with 'region-based isolation' (SE-0414), which can prove a non-`Sendable` value is no longer used by the sender after it's transferred — the value is in a disconnected region — allowing safe transfer even of non-`Sendable` types when uniqueness is provable. The `sending` keyword expresses this transfer of ownership across a boundary. `@preconcurrency` imports relax checking for code that predates Sendable annotations. The whole system is conservative: it rejects programs it can't prove safe, which is why you sometimes restructure code to satisfy it.
f: Trap: Does the compiler require every type used inside an actor to be `Sendable`? No — only values that actually cross the isolation boundary need `Sendable`; values confined to a single domain don't, and region-based isolation can even permit transferring non-Sendable values when uniqueness is provable.

q: What are global actors and how do you define a custom one?
a: A global actor is a single, process-wide actor whose isolation you can apply to many declarations across your codebase using an attribute, the way `@MainActor` works. You define one by declaring a type that conforms to the `GlobalActor` protocol, which requires a `static let shared` instance, and marking it `@globalActor`. Then any code annotated with your custom attribute is serialized on that actor's executor. This is useful for coordinating a subsystem — say, all database access — under one shared isolation domain.
d: `@MainActor` is just the built-in global actor backed by the main thread's executor. A custom global actor lets you create a shared serialized domain for, say, a rendering pipeline or a logging subsystem, so all annotated members run mutually exclusive without passing an instance around. The `shared` instance provides the executor; by default it's a fresh actor with a default serial executor, but you can supply a custom executor via the `sharedUnownedExecutor`/custom executor mechanism to control which thread or dispatch queue backs it. Because everything annotated runs on one executor, overusing a global actor can create a serialization bottleneck — it's essentially one big lock for everything wearing the attribute. Global actor isolation participates in `Sendable` checking just like instance actor isolation. They're best for genuinely singleton subsystems, not as a lazy way to make unrelated code 'thread-safe'.
f: Trap: Does putting many unrelated types on the same custom global actor improve concurrency? No — it serializes all of them onto one executor, which can become a bottleneck; global actors are one shared isolation domain, not a parallelism boost.

q: What is @preconcurrency and when do you use it?
a: `@preconcurrency` tells the compiler that a declaration or import comes from code written before Swift Concurrency's `Sendable` and isolation rules, so it should relax strict checking against it. On an import, it silences `Sendable` warnings for types from a module that hasn't been annotated yet. On a protocol or function, it preserves source compatibility while you adopt concurrency annotations incrementally. It's a migration tool that lets you move to strict concurrency without your dependencies blocking you.
d: When you import a framework with `@preconcurrency import Foo`, the compiler treats its unannotated types as if they might be `Sendable`, downgrading what would be errors to warnings or suppressing them, so you don't get flooded by violations you can't fix in someone else's code. On a protocol declaration, `@preconcurrency` lets conformers adopt stricter isolation than the original protocol promised without breaking existing callers. It also appears on function declarations to mark `Sendable` requirements as soft for pre-existing clients. The key is that it's a bridge, not a fix — it defers checking rather than proving safety, so misuse can hide real races. As the ecosystem annotates its APIs, you remove these. It pairs with the staged rollout of strict concurrency (warnings-first, then Swift 6 errors) so large codebases can migrate file by file.
f: Trap: Does `@preconcurrency import` make a non-Sendable third-party type actually safe to send across actors? No — it only silences the diagnostic; the runtime safety is still your responsibility, so you can introduce a real data race the compiler no longer warns about.

q: How do you bridge a completion-handler API to async with continuations?
a: You wrap the callback-based call in `withCheckedContinuation` or `withCheckedThrowingContinuation`, which gives you a continuation object and suspends the async function. Inside the callback, you call `continuation.resume(returning:)` on success or `resume(throwing:)` on failure, which resumes the suspended function with that result. The absolutely critical rule is that you must call resume exactly once on every path — never zero times, never twice. `withCheckedContinuation` adds runtime checks that trap if you violate this, while `withUnsafeContinuation` skips the checks for performance.
d: A continuation captures the suspended task's resumption point as a value you invoke later. The 'exactly once' rule is fundamental: resuming zero times leaks the task forever as a permanent suspension, and resuming twice is undefined behavior. The checked variants detect double-resume (trap) and dropped continuations (a runtime warning when deallocated unused), making them the default for development; switch to `withUnsafeContinuation` only after verifying correctness, for hot paths. A common bug is a callback API that can fire more than once — like a delegate that calls back per event — which you must not bridge with a single-shot continuation; use `AsyncStream` instead. You also need to handle cancellation: combine with `withTaskCancellationHandler` so cancelling the task cancels the underlying operation, then resume with a `CancellationError`. The continuation is `Sendable`-aware and can be resumed from any thread, but only once.
f: Trap: Can you use a single `withCheckedContinuation` to bridge a delegate callback that fires multiple times? No — a continuation resumes exactly once; a multi-fire callback needs `AsyncStream`, or you'll crash on the second resume.

q: What is MainActor.run and how does it differ from a @MainActor annotation?
a: `MainActor.run` is a function that runs a closure on the main actor and `await`s its result from a non-isolated context. You use it imperatively when you're in some background context and need to do a chunk of work on the main actor right now, without restructuring the whole function's isolation. A `@MainActor` annotation, by contrast, is declarative — it makes an entire function, type, or property statically isolated to the main actor. So the annotation is the preferred, compile-time-checked approach, while `MainActor.run` is an inline, dynamic hop.
d: `MainActor.run { }` is essentially `await` plus an actor hop wrapped in a convenience API; it submits the closure to the main actor's executor and suspends until it completes. Prefer static `@MainActor` isolation because it lets the compiler verify and optimize, and it documents intent; reach for `MainActor.run` only at integration points where you can't annotate, such as inside a detached task or a non-isolated callback. A related primitive is `MainActor.assumeIsolated`, which is synchronous and does not hop — it asserts you're already on the main actor and traps otherwise, useful for satisfying isolation from a context you know is on main. Overusing `MainActor.run` sprinkles runtime hops and can mask design problems where isolation should have been declared. Note `MainActor.run`'s closure is `@MainActor`-isolated, so the values you capture and return are checked for `Sendable` across the boundary.
f: Trap: Is `MainActor.run` the same as `MainActor.assumeIsolated`? No — `run` asynchronously hops to the main actor and may suspend; `assumeIsolated` is synchronous, performs no hop, and traps if you're not already on the main actor.

q: What is actor hopping and what does it cost?
a: Actor hopping is the switch the runtime performs when execution moves from one isolation domain to another — for example, a non-isolated task awaiting an actor method, which enqueues the work on that actor's executor. Each hop involves a suspension and a re-schedule onto a different executor, so it has real overhead and is a potential thread switch. The cost is usually small but adds up if you ping-pong frequently between actors in a tight loop. Minimizing hops — by batching work in one domain — is a common performance optimization.
d: When you `await` across actors, the current task suspends, the continuation is enqueued on the target actor's executor, and a thread from the pool eventually runs it; if the target is the main actor it may require switching to the main thread. The runtime optimizes some cases — if you're already on the target actor, no hop occurs. Frequent hopping shows up as scheduling overhead and latency, and in the worst case can cause executor contention. A classic anti-pattern is calling many small main-actor methods from a background loop, each forcing a hop to the main thread; the fix is to gather the data and make one batched main-actor call. Custom executors can reduce hop cost in specialized cases by aligning work onto a desired thread. The mental model: an `await` across isolation is not free, so design APIs to transfer data in larger units rather than chatty round-trips.
f: Trap: Is calling an actor method ten times in a loop just as cheap as calling it once with a batch? No — each call across the boundary can incur a hop and suspension; batching into one call avoids repeated scheduling overhead.

q: What are isolated parameters and why are they useful?
a: An `isolated` parameter lets a function declare that it runs within the isolation of an actor instance passed as an argument, written like `func f(on actor: isolated MyActor)`. Inside the function body, you can synchronously access that actor's isolated state without `await`, because the function itself is isolated to it. This is powerful for writing generic helpers that operate on any actor's state, or for avoiding repeated awaits when you do many operations on one actor. It moves the hop to the call site rather than each access.
d: Normally each access to an actor's isolated member from outside requires `await`; an `isolated` parameter hoists that isolation into the function, so the function executes on the actor's executor and all interior accesses are synchronous. The caller pays one hop to enter the function. There can be at most one `isolated` parameter, since a function executes in a single isolation domain. This enables nonmember functions that behave like methods on the actor, and it's how `MainActor`-isolated free functions can be written generically. It also underpins patterns where you want to perform a transaction-like sequence on an actor without interleaving from other awaits inside the helper — though reentrancy still applies if the helper itself awaits. A related feature, `#isolation`, captures the caller's isolation as a default for `isolated (any Actor)?` parameters, used by APIs like `withTaskGroup` ordering helpers. It's an advanced tool but key for ergonomic actor APIs.
f: Trap: Can a function have two `isolated` parameters to access two actors synchronously? No — a function runs in exactly one isolation domain, so only one `isolated` parameter is allowed; the second actor still requires `await`.

q: What is a custom executor and why might you implement one?
a: An executor is the object that actually runs the jobs scheduled by tasks and actors; the default ones are the cooperative thread pool and the main thread. A custom executor lets you control where and how an actor's or task's work runs — for instance, pinning an actor's work to a specific dispatch queue, a dedicated thread, or coalescing for a real-time audio context. You implement `SerialExecutor` (for actors) or `TaskExecutor`, providing an `enqueue` method, and have your actor return it from `unownedExecutor`. It's an advanced tool for integrating Swift Concurrency with existing threading requirements.
d: Actors use a `SerialExecutor` to guarantee serialized execution; by default each actor gets one backed by the cooperative pool. By conforming a type to `SerialExecutor` and returning it from an actor's `nonisolated var unownedExecutor`, you redirect that actor's jobs to, say, a specific `DispatchQueue`, which is useful when interacting with APIs that demand a particular thread (some C libraries, Core Data contexts, audio). `TaskExecutor` (SE-0417) lets you set a preferred executor for a task tree, influencing where non-actor-isolated async work runs without changing isolation semantics. Implementing executors correctly requires honoring the `enqueue(_:)` contract with `ExecutorJob`/`UnownedJob` and, for serial executors, supporting `checkIsolated`/`isSameExclusiveExecutionContext` so the runtime can verify isolation and optimize hops. Misimplementing serialization reintroduces data races, so it's expert territory. The payoff is bridging concurrency to environments with strict thread affinity while keeping the actor model's safety.
f: Trap: Does a custom `SerialExecutor` that dispatches to a concurrent queue still give actor safety? No — a serial executor must run jobs one at a time; backing it with a concurrent queue breaks the actor's mutual-exclusion guarantee and reintroduces data races.

q: What is AsyncSequence and how does AsyncStream relate to it?
a: `AsyncSequence` is the asynchronous analog of `Sequence` — you iterate it with `for await element in seq`, and each iteration can suspend while the next element is produced. `AsyncStream` is a concrete, ready-made way to build an `AsyncSequence` from a callback or push-based source: you get a continuation and `yield` values into it, then `finish` when done. So `AsyncSequence` is the protocol you consume, and `AsyncStream` is the bridge you use to turn delegate callbacks, notifications, or timers into something you can `for await` over.
d: `AsyncSequence` requires an `AsyncIterator` whose `next()` is `async throws`, enabling backpressure-free pull-based iteration where consumption drives production. `AsyncStream` comes in throwing and non-throwing forms and offers a buffering policy — `.unbounded`, `.bufferingNewest(n)`, or `.bufferingOldest(n)` — to control what happens when production outpaces consumption, which is your main tool for managing memory and backpressure. You feed it via `continuation.yield(_:)` and signal completion with `continuation.finish()`, and you set `continuation.onTermination` to clean up the underlying source when the consumer stops or the stream is cancelled. A subtlety: a single `AsyncStream` typically supports one consumer; for multicast you need a different design. Cancellation of the consuming task ends the `for await` loop and triggers `onTermination`. Operators like `map`, `filter`, and `prefix` exist via the standard library and `swift-async-algorithms`. This is the idiomatic replacement for callback-based event streams and a key contrast with Combine's `Publisher`.
f: Trap: Can multiple tasks safely iterate the same `AsyncStream` to fan out events to several consumers? No — `AsyncStream` is single-consumer; iterating from multiple tasks splits elements unpredictably rather than broadcasting; you need a multicast/broadcast abstraction instead.

q: How does async/await compare to Combine?
a: Async/await is a language-level model for sequential asynchronous code with structured concurrency, cancellation, and compile-time data-race safety built in. Combine is a reactive framework built around `Publisher`s and `Subscriber`s with a rich set of operators for composing streams of values over time. For one-shot asynchronous operations, async/await is simpler and safer; for complex event streams with operators like `debounce`, `combineLatest`, and `merge`, Combine — or `AsyncSequence` with swift-async-algorithms — fits better. The industry direction is toward async/await and `AsyncSequence`, with Combine increasingly in maintenance mode.
d: Combine models everything as streams with explicit demand/backpressure via `Subscribers.Demand`, and its operators are declarative but can be hard to debug due to deep type erasure (`AnyPublisher`) and retain-cycle pitfalls with subscriptions. Async/await integrates with the cooperative pool, gives structured cancellation that propagates through the task tree, and is checked by the `Sendable`/isolation system, which Combine predates and doesn't enforce as tightly. Bridging exists: `Publisher.values` exposes a publisher as an `AsyncSequence`, and you can wrap async work in a `Future`. Combine remains strong for UI-bound reactive pipelines, especially with SwiftUI's `@Published`, though SwiftUI increasingly uses the Observation framework instead. Combine is Apple-platform-only and not open source, while Swift Concurrency is part of the language and cross-platform. For new code, prefer async/await for tasks and `AsyncStream`/async-algorithms for streams, reserving Combine for existing reactive architectures.
f: Trap: Does using `Publisher.values` to bridge Combine to async give you the same cancellation semantics as native async code? Mostly — cancelling the consuming task cancels the subscription, but you still inherit Combine's backpressure and completion semantics, so behavior under high throughput can differ from a native `AsyncStream`.

q: How do actors compare to serial dispatch queues for protecting state?
a: Both serialize access so only one operation touches the state at a time, but actors do it with compile-time enforcement and non-blocking waits, while serial queues are a runtime convention that can block threads. With an actor, the compiler prevents you from accessing isolated state unsafely, and a task waiting on a busy actor suspends rather than blocking a thread. A serial queue using `sync` blocks the caller's thread and can deadlock if you re-enter it. The big behavioral difference is that actors are reentrant across `await`, whereas a serial queue running synchronous blocks is not interruptible mid-block.
d: A serial `DispatchQueue` gives you a single sequential execution context, and synchronous blocks on it are atomic from start to finish — nothing interleaves. Actors only guarantee atomicity within synchronous spans between suspension points; at every `await` the actor can process other work, so a method spanning awaits is not atomic. This means migrating queue-based code to an actor can silently introduce reentrancy bugs if the old code relied on a whole block being uninterrupted. On the safety side, actors win decisively: the compiler enforces isolation and `Sendable`, eliminating data races by construction, whereas a serial queue offers no such checking and is easy to bypass. On performance, actors integrate with the cooperative pool and avoid thread blocking, reducing thread explosion risk. Choose actors for new code, but understand the reentrancy semantics before assuming a drop-in replacement.
f: Trap: Is migrating a serial-queue-protected method to an `actor` method always behavior-preserving? No — a synchronous queue block is fully atomic, but an actor method with an `await` inside can be interrupted by reentrancy, changing semantics.

q: What does atomicity mean in concurrency and why is an atomic counter not enough?
a: Atomicity means an operation appears indivisible to other threads — it either fully happens or not at all, with no observable intermediate state. An atomic counter guarantees that a single increment is safe and won't tear, but it doesn't make a compound operation safe. If you read the counter, do something based on the value, then write back, that read-modify-decide-write sequence isn't atomic as a whole, so another thread can interleave and you get a race condition. Atomicity of individual operations doesn't compose into atomicity of sequences.
d: Hardware atomics guarantee single load/store/compare-exchange operations are indivisible and have defined memory ordering, which prevents data races on that location. But correctness usually requires multi-step invariants — for example, 'if count is below the limit, increment it' is a check-then-act that needs the check and act to be one atomic unit, typically via compare-and-swap in a loop or a lock. Using an atomic for the counter but a separate non-atomic decision reintroduces a race condition even though there's no data race. This is the classic 'atomic operations don't make algorithms atomic' lesson. Swift's `Atomic<T>` (from the Synchronization module) provides typed atomics with explicit memory orderings for lock-free code, but you still need CAS loops for compound updates. For most app code, a lock or actor expressing the larger critical section is clearer and less error-prone than hand-rolled atomics.
f: Trap: If every shared variable is an `Atomic`, is your code free of race conditions? No — atomics prevent data races on individual accesses, but compound operations like check-then-act across multiple atomics still race; you need a single critical section.

q: Compare NSLock, os_unfair_lock, and OSAllocatedUnfairLock.
a: `NSLock` is an Objective-C, object-based mutex — easy to use and supporting features like `lock(before:)`, but heavier and with some overhead. `os_unfair_lock` is a lightweight, low-level primitive that's very fast but unfair, meaning it doesn't guarantee FIFO ordering and can favor whoever grabs it. `OSAllocatedUnfairLock` is the modern Swift-friendly wrapper around `os_unfair_lock` that fixes a critical safety problem: the raw `os_unfair_lock` must not be copied, and Swift's value semantics make that easy to do accidentally. For new Swift code protecting a small critical section, `OSAllocatedUnfairLock` is the recommended choice.
d: `os_unfair_lock` replaced the deprecated `OSSpinLock`, which was unsafe under QoS because spinning could cause priority inversion; `os_unfair_lock` instead parks waiters and participates in priority donation. Its 'unfair' nature trades fairness for performance and is fine for short critical sections. The hazard in Swift is that `os_unfair_lock` is a struct that must remain at a stable address — passing it by value or storing it in a Swift type that gets copied corrupts it — so Apple added `OSAllocatedUnfairLock`, which heap-allocates the lock and offers a safe `withLock` closure API, plus a generic state-protecting variant `OSAllocatedUnfairLock<State>`. `NSLock` conforms to `NSLocking` and is reentrancy-unsafe (use `NSRecursiveLock` if you need recursion). All of these block threads, so within Swift Concurrency you generally prefer actors; locks are appropriate for synchronous, fine-grained protection or `@unchecked Sendable` types. Always pair lock with unlock on every path, ideally via a `withLock`/`defer` pattern to avoid leaks on early return or throw.
f: Trap: Is it safe to store an `os_unfair_lock` as a `var` property of a Swift `struct`? No — it must not be copied or moved; copying breaks it. Use `OSAllocatedUnfairLock` (heap-backed) or a class with a stable address instead.

q: How do you implement thread-safe lazy initialization in Swift?
a: For most cases, a `static let` global or static property is the simplest correct answer — Swift guarantees it's initialized exactly once, lazily, in a thread-safe way using `dispatch_once` semantics under the hood. A non-static `lazy var` is NOT thread-safe; concurrent first accesses can run the initializer multiple times or race. If you need lazy instance-level initialization with thread safety, you protect it with a lock, perform it inside an actor, or use a `static` factory. So the rule of thumb is: `static let` for singletons, and explicit synchronization for anything lazier than that.
d: Swift's `static let` and global `let` initialization is backed by `swift_once`, which uses `dispatch_once`-style one-time execution, so even under heavy concurrent first access the initializer runs exactly once and other threads block until it completes. This is the canonical safe singleton. By contrast, `lazy var` on a class or struct has no synchronization — two threads hitting it simultaneously before it's set can both compute the value, and on a struct it's even a mutating access, which is unsafe to share. The fix for instance-level laziness is to guard the backing optional with an `os_unfair_lock`/`OSAllocatedUnfairLock` (double-checked or single-checked under the lock) or to make the owner an actor so access is serialized. Double-checked locking in Swift must be done carefully with proper synchronization on both the check and the set, since the language doesn't give you the volatile/acquire-release guarantees for free outside of atomics. When in doubt, prefer `static let` or an actor over hand-rolled lazy patterns.
f: Trap: Is `lazy var cache = expensiveComputation()` thread-safe for concurrent first access? No — `lazy var` has no synchronization, so concurrent access can run the initializer multiple times; use `static let` or guard it with a lock/actor.

q: What are the basics of memory ordering and why does it matter?
a: Memory ordering governs how reads and writes to memory by one thread become visible to others, because both compilers and CPUs reorder operations for performance. Without proper ordering, one thread can see writes in a different order than another performed them, breaking assumptions like 'the flag is set, so the data must be ready'. Synchronization primitives — locks, atomics with acquire/release semantics — establish happens-before relationships that guarantee visibility and ordering. In most app code you rely on locks and actors to handle this, but with lock-free atomics you must specify orderings explicitly.
d: The relevant model is sequential consistency versus relaxed orderings. `relaxed` atomics guarantee atomicity but no ordering with respect to other memory operations; `acquire` on a load and `release` on a store create a synchronizes-with edge so that everything written before the release is visible after the matching acquire; `seq_cst` gives a single total order at higher cost. The classic bug is publishing data with a relaxed flag write — the reader sees the flag set but the data writes may not yet be visible, because nothing forced ordering. Swift's `Atomic` type (Synchronization module) requires you to pass an ordering, mirroring C++'s memory model. Locks and actors provide full barriers, so code inside a critical section enjoys acquire/release semantics automatically — which is why high-level synchronization is safer than hand-rolled atomics. Misunderstanding ordering is a leading cause of subtle, hardware-dependent lock-free bugs that may never reproduce on x86 but fail on ARM, which has a weaker memory model.
f: Trap: With relaxed atomics, if thread A writes data then sets an atomic flag, is thread B guaranteed to see the data once it sees the flag? No — relaxed ordering provides no happens-before for the surrounding writes; you need release/acquire ordering to guarantee the data is visible.

q: What is a @TaskLocal value and how does it propagate?
a: A `@TaskLocal` is a property that carries contextual data through a task and its child tasks — things like a request ID, a logger, or a user context — without threading it manually through every function signature. You declare it as a static property with `@TaskLocal`, then bind a value for the duration of a scope using `$myLocal.withValue(x) { ... }`, and any code in that scope, including child tasks, reads the bound value. It's the structured-concurrency answer to thread-local storage, but scoped to the task tree rather than to a thread.
d: Task-locals are immutable within a binding scope; you don't assign to them directly, you bind them around a closure with `withValue`, and the value reverts when the scope exits. Propagation follows the structured task tree: child tasks created with `async let` or `withTaskGroup`, and plain `Task { }` (which inherits context), see the bound values, whereas `Task.detached` does NOT inherit them because it severs context. This makes them ideal for cross-cutting concerns where you'd otherwise pass a parameter everywhere — distributed tracing libraries like swift-distributed-tracing rely on them. Reads are cheap and copy-on-bind; the binding is a stack-like push/pop tied to the scope. A subtlety: because detached tasks don't inherit, you must rebind explicitly if you spawn detached work that needs the context. They're a clean alternative to thread-local storage, which doesn't map well to a model where a task can hop threads.
f: Trap: Does a `Task.detached` inherit the surrounding `@TaskLocal` values? No — detached tasks sever context, so they don't inherit task-locals; only `Task { }` and structured children do.

q: What is priority escalation in Swift Concurrency?
a: Priority escalation is the runtime's mechanism for raising the effective priority of a task when a higher-priority task is waiting on it, to avoid priority inversion. If a high-priority task awaits the result of a lower-priority child task, or waits on an actor whose current work is low priority, the system boosts that lower work so it finishes sooner and unblocks the waiter. This happens automatically through the structured task tree and actor scheduling. It's the modern, runtime-aware equivalent of the QoS donation GCD does with `sync`.
d: Because structured concurrency makes dependencies explicit — a parent awaits its children, a caller awaits an actor — the runtime can see who's waiting on whom and escalate accordingly. When a high-priority task awaits a `Task` or a child, that task's priority is raised for the duration; when it completes or the waiter goes away, priority can drop back. Actors participate too: enqueuing a high-priority job can escalate the actor's currently running lower-priority job. This is exactly why blocking primitives like `DispatchSemaphore` are harmful — they hide the dependency, so the runtime can't escalate and you get an inversion. `Task.currentPriority` reflects the possibly-escalated value. Escalation is one-directional in spirit: priorities go up to relieve inversions, they don't get arbitrarily lowered. Designing with structured concurrency lets the system manage this for you, which is a strong argument for preferring it over manual threading where the runtime is blind to dependencies.
f: Trap: Does priority escalation work when a high-priority task waits on a low-priority task via a `DispatchSemaphore`? No — the semaphore hides the dependency from the runtime, so no escalation occurs and you get a priority inversion; use `await` on the task instead.

q: How does Sendable checking apply to classes, and how do you make a class Sendable?
a: Classes are reference types, so sharing one across concurrency domains means multiple domains can mutate the same instance — inherently race-prone — which is why classes aren't `Sendable` by default. A class can be safely `Sendable` only if it's immutable: `final` with all `let` properties of `Sendable` type. If the class has mutable state but you protect it with internal synchronization like a lock, you can declare `@unchecked Sendable` to assert safety the compiler can't verify. Otherwise, the better path is usually to use a `struct` or an `actor` instead.
d: For a `final class` to be implicitly or explicitly `Sendable`, every stored property must be immutable and `Sendable`, and the class must not be open to subclassing that could add mutable state (hence `final`). Non-final classes generally can't be `Sendable` because a subclass could violate the invariant. When you need mutable shared state in a class — say a cache wrapped in `OSAllocatedUnfairLock` — `@unchecked Sendable` tells the compiler to trust your manual synchronization; this is a deliberate, auditable escape hatch, not a free pass, and a mistake here reintroduces data races silently. `@unchecked Sendable` is common in framework code bridging to older patterns. The cleanest modern alternatives are actors (always `Sendable`, compiler-enforced) and value types. Under Swift 6 strict checking, passing a non-`Sendable` class across an isolation boundary is a hard error, pushing you toward one of these solutions or `sending`/region-based isolation if uniqueness can be proven.
f: Trap: Is a `class` with all `let` properties automatically `Sendable`? Only if it's also `final` (or otherwise not subclassable to add mutable state) and every property type is `Sendable`; a non-final class generally can't be implicitly `Sendable`.

q: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
a: Concurrency is about structure — dealing with multiple tasks that are in progress at overlapping times, which you can do even on a single core by interleaving them. Parallelism is about execution — actually running multiple tasks at the same instant, which requires multiple cores. Concurrency is a way of composing independent work so it can make progress without waiting; parallelism is one possible way that concurrent work gets executed when hardware allows. You can have concurrency without parallelism, like async I/O on one core, and the two are distinct concerns.
d: Rob Pike's framing captures it: concurrency is dealing with many things at once, parallelism is doing many things at once. Swift Concurrency's async/await is fundamentally about concurrency — it lets many tasks be suspended and resumed efficiently so a small thread pool handles many in-flight operations, which is ideal for I/O-bound work that mostly waits. Parallelism comes into play for CPU-bound work, where you want multiple tasks genuinely running on multiple cores simultaneously, achieved via the cooperative pool spreading runnable tasks across cores or constructs like `withTaskGroup` for data parallelism. A single-core device still benefits from concurrency because while one task awaits the network, another can run. Conflating them leads to wrong expectations — adding more async tasks won't speed up CPU-bound work beyond core count, and using concurrency for parallelism requires actually-parallel-friendly work. The cooperative pool intentionally caps threads near core count precisely because more threads don't add parallelism, only overhead.
f: Trap: Will splitting a CPU-bound computation into many async tasks make it run faster on a single-core device? No — concurrency interleaves but doesn't add parallelism; with one core you get no speedup and pay scheduling overhead.

q: Why might you still need DispatchQueue.main.async even when already on the main thread?
a: Sometimes you're on the main thread but in the middle of an operation where you need to defer work until the current run-loop cycle finishes — for example, updating UI after the current layout pass, avoiding reentrancy into a method that's still executing, or letting an animation or table-view update settle before reacting. `DispatchQueue.main.async` from the main thread schedules the block to run later on a subsequent run-loop iteration, rather than immediately. So it's not about getting onto the main thread; it's about ordering and breaking out of the current call stack. It's a way to say 'do this after everything currently in flight completes'.
d: Even though you're already on the main thread, your code is executing synchronously within some call; `async` enqueues onto the main queue so the block runs after the current work item and run-loop processing yields. Classic uses: deferring a `becomeFirstResponder` or a UI mutation until after `viewDidLoad`/layout completes, avoiding 'modifying state during view update' issues, breaking a reentrancy cycle where a delegate callback would otherwise re-enter a method mid-execution, and coalescing so multiple synchronous triggers result in one deferred action. It also lets the current animation transaction commit before the next change. The mental model is that `main.async` from main is a 'next tick' / 'do later' primitive, analogous to a zero-delay defer. Overusing it to paper over ordering bugs is a smell, but it's a legitimate and sometimes necessary tool. In Swift Concurrency, an equivalent intent is sometimes expressed with `Task { @MainActor in ... }` or yielding, though the run-loop semantics differ.
f: Trap: Is `DispatchQueue.main.async` from the main thread a no-op since you're already on main? No — it doesn't run inline; it defers the block to a later run-loop turn, which is the whole point when you need 'after the current work completes' ordering.

q: Why is it dangerous to hold an invariant across a suspension point?
a: An `await` is a point where your function can suspend and other code — including other calls into the same actor — can run before you resume. If you established some invariant before the `await` and assume it still holds afterward, you can be wrong, because the world may have changed during the suspension: state mutated, resources released, the task cancelled. This is the root cause of actor reentrancy bugs and many subtle concurrency errors. The discipline is to never assume anything you read or set before an `await` is still valid after it without re-checking.
d: Conceptually, treat every `await` as 'arbitrary other code may execute here, and I may resume on a different thread'. Concretely: a value you cached, a 'currently loading' flag you set, an index into a collection that another call may have mutated, or an assumption that you still hold a logical lock can all be invalidated. Even non-actor async code is exposed — if you read a shared property before awaiting and write it after, you've created a check-then-act race. The correct pattern is to perform validation and mutation in the synchronous spans between suspension points, re-read state after awaiting, and store in-flight `Task`s to deduplicate rather than relying on flags. Cancellation can also occur across the await, so resources acquired before may need cleanup. This is why the conceptual model of 'awaits are tears in atomicity' is so important for senior engineers; it reframes async functions as sequences of small atomic regions rather than one continuous atomic block.
f: Trap: If you check `if !isLoading { isLoading = true }` then `await fetch()`, is the duplicate-request guard sound? No — between setting the flag and awaiting, the flag is fine, but the broader pattern still risks reentrancy if you re-check stale state after the await; the robust fix stores and awaits an in-flight `Task` rather than a boolean.
